sector: Industrials — Specialty Mechanical & Electrical Construction / Facilities Services
bull_thesis:
EMCOR is one of the purest, highest-quality publicly traded picks-and-shovels plays on the physical AI build-out: the company installs the electrical distribution, mechanical systems, HVAC (heating, ventilation, and air conditioning), piping, and fire and life-safety systems inside hyperscaler data centers — the exact thermal and electrical infrastructure that every dollar of AI capital spending must pay for.

The first quarter of 2026 was a clean confirmation of the thesis at work. Revenue came in at $4.63 billion, up 19.7% year over year, with organic growth (growth excluding acquisitions) of 16.8% — genuine demand, not roll-up arithmetic. US Electrical revenue grew 33.1% and US Mechanical grew 28.9%, with the network and communications line up roughly 50% in Electrical and 86% in Mechanical.

Forward visibility is the standout feature. The remaining performance obligation, or RPO (the total value of work under contract not yet recognized as revenue), hit a record $15.62 billion, up 32.9% year over year and $2.37 billion higher than the prior quarter. Book-to-bill (new orders divided by revenue recognized) is comfortably above 1, and roughly 78% of that backlog converts to revenue within 12 months — meaning 2026 is largely already contracted and 2027 already has a $6 to $6.5 billion head start.

The financial model is elite. Operating margin expanded to 8.7%, operating income grew 26.7% (faster than revenue), earnings per share rose 30%, return on invested capital runs at roughly 21 to 27%, and free cash flow was approximately $1.34 billion in 2024. The balance sheet is pristine and effectively net-cash, with $916 million in cash against only $6.1 million of total debt and a debt-to-capital ratio of 0.2%.

Management raised full-year guidance (revenue to $18.50 to $19.25 billion, earnings per share to $28.25 to $29.75) and was candid about the one genuine constraint: supervision capacity, not demand. Capital allocation is shareholder-friendly — 51% of cash deployed to buybacks and dividends since 2017, steadily shrinking the share count and compounding per-share earnings. The UK divestiture sharpens focus on the US data-center opportunity.

The bull case: AI and cloud data-center capital spending stays elevated into 2027 and beyond, the backlog keeps converting at high incremental margins, EMCOR compounds earnings per share in the high-teens to low-20s percent range, and the net-cash war chest funds bolt-on acquisitions and buybacks — a durable compounder riding the largest non-residential construction wave in a generation.
bear_thesis:
EMCOR is a great business at a full price, and the AI capital-spending optionality is substantially already reflected in the stock. After a roughly 109% rally over twelve months, it trades at approximately 26 times trailing and 25 times forward earnings — well above its five-year median of roughly 17 times and the contractor-group average of roughly 23 times. The stock actually fell about 1.8% on the record beat-and-raise quarter, which is the classic signal that good news is already priced in.

The competitive moat is narrow. Specialty construction is a project-by-project, re-compete business with no recurring contractual lock-in on the construction side. EMCOR's edge comes from scale, bonding capacity (the ability to post financial guarantees on large projects), execution reputation, and a deep supervision bench — not from customer switching costs.

The most important driver — hyperscaler AI and data-center capital spending — is a cycle, not an annuity. If that spending normalizes or pauses after 2027, the two construction segments (which now carry the company) de-rate hard, and the stock's multiple compresses on both lower growth and a lower exit multiple simultaneously.

Margins are already showing the cost of scaling. Mechanical margin compressed 100 basis points (from 11.9% to 10.9%) on guaranteed-maximum-price and cost-plus work in newer geographies. Management's pivot to talking about 'margin dollars, not margin points' pre-positions investors for percentage-margin dilution ahead. Customer concentration in a handful of hyperscalers is not disclosed — a single client's capex pause could dent the bookings rate.

Within the scale-out basket, EMCOR's thesis rests on the same shared macro driver (AI capital-spending durability) as every other name in the same group, adding little independent diversification.

The bear case: a fully valued cyclical at a cycle-favorable peak, where any wobble in AI capital spending would compress both earnings growth and the earnings multiple at the same time.
sop_bull: The valuation here is a straightforward earnings-multiple call on a single, integrated operating company — not a sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) exercise, because the mechanical and electrical segments share the same platform. Using a 2027 forward earnings-per-share estimate of roughly $33 to $34 (high-teens growth as the backlog converts) and a 30 times multiple — justified by best-in-class return on invested capital of 21 to 27%, a net-cash balance sheet, and a record backlog, with peers Quanta Services (PWR) at roughly 52 times and Comfort Systems (FIX) in the high-20s to 30 times — the equity value works out to roughly $990 to $1,020 per share. The roughly $910 million net-cash position (about $20 per share) is already embedded in that equity multiple. Bull case: approximately $990 to $1,020 per share (roughly 30 times approximately $33 2027 estimated earnings per share).
sop_bear: Adverse case: assume the AI capital-spending wave decelerates and the contractor group re-rates toward its historical average multiple. Applying roughly 18 to 19 times the 2026 estimated earnings per share of approximately $29.00 — closer to EMCOR's five-year median of roughly 17 times and MasTec's (MTZ) forward multiple of roughly 18 times — produces an equity value of roughly $520 to $550 per share, with the roughly $910 million net-cash position (about $20 per share) providing a floor-supporting asset. Bear case: approximately $520 to $550 per share (roughly 18 times trough-normalized earnings).
sop_breakdown:
The base case anchors on the 2026 estimated earnings-per-share midpoint of roughly $29.00 at a roughly 26 to 27 times multiple. That premium-but-not-peak multiple is warranted by roughly 21 to 27% return on invested capital, expanding operating margins, a record $15.62 billion backlog with 78% converting within 12 months, and an effectively net-cash balance sheet — yet is discounted relative to Quanta Services (roughly 52 times) and Comfort Systems (high-20s to 30 times) to reflect EMCOR's lower secular-growth profile and the fact that its construction revenue does not recur contractually.

Roughly $29.00 times 26.5 equals approximately $770; adding modest credit for the roughly $910 million net-cash position (about $20 per share) brings the estimate to roughly $790. Rounded to roughly $760 to $800, with a midpoint of approximately $780.

The bull case ($990 to $1,020) reflects 30 times accelerating 2027 earnings per share. The bear case ($520 to $550) reflects de-rating to roughly 18 times on a return to a more normal cycle. Because this is a single, integrated operating company, there is no hidden valuation discount to unlock by splitting it up — this is a clean earnings-multiple call.
risk_reward: At roughly $845: the downside to the bear case of roughly $535 is approximately -37%; the downside to the base-case fair value of roughly $780 is approximately -8% (the stock trades above base-case fair value); the upside to the bull case of roughly $1,005 is approximately +19%. The asymmetry is unfavorable at the current price — the stock already trades through the base-case fair value, so the risk-reward is roughly -37% downside versus +19% bull-case upside, or about 0.5 to 1. A pullback to roughly $700 would restore a more balanced risk-reward.
catalyst_status: FIRED. The load-bearing catalysts have already printed and the stock has already run: record Q1 2026 (revenue +19.7%, RPO +32.9% to $15.62B) reported and the full-year guide RAISED (May 2026); the AI/data-center demand inflection is recognized, consensus, and reflected in a ~109% twelve-month rally and a ~26x multiple. Evidence it is fired, not pending: the stock fell ~1.8% on the record beat-and-raise print. The remaining 'catalyst' (continued backlog conversion through 2026, more raises) is incremental and expected, not a re-rating surprise. This is an already-ran, in-the-print name — treated as defensive/quality-compounder, NOT an asymmetric special-situation. No hard-dated NRC/PPA/design-win milestone is the driver; the driver is the same AI-capex tape the whole basket shares.
peer_comps_note:
The closest comparables are AI-infrastructure construction and engineering and construction companies: Comfort Systems USA (FIX, the nearest match — mechanical and electrical plus modular construction for data centers), Quanta Services (PWR, electric power, grid, and data center), MasTec (MTZ, infrastructure and communications), Dycom Industries (DY, telecom and fiber), and broader engineering and construction firms such as Fluor and AECOM.

Live multiples as of June 2026: EMCOR roughly 26 times trailing and 25 times forward price-to-earnings (five-year median roughly 17 times; industry average roughly 23 times); Quanta Services forward roughly 52 times (richest multiple in the group, reflecting the highest secular-growth premium); Comfort Systems trades at a premium in the high-20s to roughly 30 times; MasTec forward roughly 18 times; Dycom mid-teens.

Ranking within the group: On valuation, EMCOR sits mid-pack — notably cheaper than Quanta and Comfort Systems, more expensive than MasTec and Dycom, offering the best risk-adjusted value among the high-quality names. On growth, EMCOR's 16.8% organic growth is strong but below Quanta's secular grid story, which accounts for Quanta's richer multiple. On margins, EMCOR's 8.7% operating margin is mid-pack — Comfort Systems runs higher mechanical-mix margins — but EMCOR's consistency is an edge. On returns, EMCOR's 21 to 27% return on invested capital and pristine net-cash balance sheet are best-in-class in the group, superior to MasTec's leverage and comparable to Comfort Systems. On AI and data-center exposure, EMCOR is highly and directly exposed (network and communications revenue up 50% in Electrical and 86% in Mechanical), but Quanta and Comfort Systems are equally exposed, so EMCOR is not differentiated on that dimension alone.

Summary: EMCOR is the highest-quality, lowest-leverage name in the group at a more reasonable multiple than Quanta or Comfort Systems — but the entire cohort is a single AI capital-spending bet, which limits diversification benefit.
role_in_scaleout: EMCOR provides the hands that physically build the electrical distribution and mechanical and cooling backbone inside hyperscaler data centers — switchgear installation, power feeders, HVAC and chilled-water piping, and fire and life-safety systems — the non-deferrable, on-site labor that every AI facility requires. Demand is highly durable through roughly 2027 (contracted in a record $15.62 billion backlog), but it is cyclical capital spending, not a recurring annuity, beyond the current build wave.
consensus_delta:
There is almost no positive divergence from market consensus remaining — the street has fully embraced the AI and data-center thesis for EMCOR (the stock is up roughly 109% over twelve months at roughly 26 times earnings). Where the market may be looking the wrong way is on the downside, not the upside.

First, consensus extrapolates the roughly 33% and 29% growth rates in Electrical and Mechanical construction and projects high-teens earnings-per-share compounding deep into 2027 and beyond — but the supervision bottleneck management itself flagged ('we have to create more foreman') caps conversion velocity, and the 78% twelve-month conversion rate (down from 82%) signals the backlog tail is lengthening. Growth may decelerate before consensus models it.

Second, the street rewards the percentage-margin story, but Mechanical margin already slipped 100 basis points on guaranteed-maximum-price and new-geography mix, and management is steering toward 'margin dollars, not margin points' — a tell that gross-margin percentage drifts sideways to down as the company scales.

Third, and most important: the exit multiple. A roughly 26 times multiple on a no-recurring-revenue, project-re-compete cyclical assumes the AI capital-spending cycle is secular. If it proves cyclical, both earnings growth and the multiple compress together. The place where we disagree with the market is negative — the good news is in the price.
valley_of_death:
EMCOR has no balance-sheet vulnerability. It is effectively net-cash (roughly $910 million of cash against $6.1 million of debt), generates more than $1.3 billion of free cash flow, and faces no maturity wall or cash-burn hump; solvency risk is essentially nil.

The near-term risk (three to nine months) is entirely about valuation and sentiment tied to capital-spending cadence. The stock trades above its base-case fair value of roughly $780 at roughly $845, so any wobble in AI capital spending — a single hyperscaler trimming or pausing a build, a data-center power or permitting bottleneck headline, or a broad market de-risking where AI capital spending accounts for roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings growth — hits this richly priced cyclical hard and early.

Further, the upcoming quarterly earnings (second and third quarter 2026) must continue to beat and raise just to hold the current multiple; a merely in-line quarter, or a Mechanical-margin slip, likely de-rates the stock even with strong absolute results — the stock already fell on a record beat. Near-term mix headwinds from guaranteed-maximum-price and new-geography work will continue to dilute percentage margins.

The risky window here is a multiple-compression air pocket, not a fundamental break in the business.
positioning_washout:
There is no dividend-cut or business-pivot dynamic that would force selling. EMCOR pays a modest dividend (roughly 5% of cash returns; 95% goes to buybacks), so there is no yield-focused investor base that would be forced to liquidate on a dividend change.

The relevant dynamic runs the other way. EMCOR is a crowded, momentum-owned, AI-capital-spending-beta name held by growth, quality, and thematic AI-infrastructure funds. The forced-selling risk is a momentum unwind: if AI capital-spending sentiment cracks, EMCOR sits in the same de-risking bucket as Quanta, Comfort Systems, and MasTec, and the whole cohort sells off together mechanically, regardless of EMCOR's superior balance sheet.

There is no forced-selling catalyst to buy into as a contrarian opportunity; rather, the shareholder base is a liability in a risk-off market. There is also no activist angle — no valuation discount to unlock by splitting up a clean single-operating-company, and the net-cash position is already being efficiently returned to shareholders.
forcing_function:
There is no positive catalyst that re-rates EMCOR higher — the re-rating already happened. The dated events that matter now are downside or maintenance tests: the second-quarter 2026 earnings print (expected late July 2026) and the third-quarter print (expected late October 2026), each of which must beat and raise to defend the roughly 26 times multiple; the half-year backlog and remaining-performance-obligation update (does book-to-bill stay above 1, and does the 12-month conversion rate hold or slip further from 78%); and any macro AI capital-spending signal (a hyperscaler capital-expenditure guide-down is the named systemic risk).

The honest read: the catalyst is already fired, so the next dated events are tests the stock must pass to hold its price, not surprises that force a higher re-rating. The asymmetry has flipped to the downside.
moderator_conclusion:
EMCOR is the highest-quality, cleanest-balance-sheet name in the scale-out thermal and electrical contractor group, and the forensic record is impeccable: organic growth of 16.8%, remaining performance obligation up 32.9% to a record $15.62 billion (with 78% converting within 12 months), operating margin expanding to 8.7%, return on invested capital of roughly 21 to 27%, an effectively net-cash balance sheet, earnings per share up 30%, guidance raised, and a candid, seasoned management team (management credibility 5 of 5; trajectory strengthening). If this were a verdict on business quality alone it would be a clear buy.

But the mandate here covers timing, saturation, and margin of safety — and on all three the answer is the same: the AI capital-spending optionality is already in the price. The stock is up roughly 109% over twelve months at roughly 26 times trailing and 25 times forward earnings (versus a five-year median of roughly 17 times). It fell about 1.8% on a record beat-and-raise — the textbook signal that good news is priced in. And it trades above the base-case fair value of roughly $760 to $800 (midpoint roughly $780) at roughly $845, with risk-reward of roughly -37% to the bear case versus +19% to the bull case — an unfavorable ratio of about 0.5 to 1.

The load-bearing catalyst is already fired, not pending. The stock's single driver — hyperscaler AI capital-spending durability — is the same shared macro factor as the entire basket, the named systemic risk, and adds no independent diversification to the book.

The moat is narrow (project-re-compete, no recurring lock-in) though widening in returns terms, and the secular threat is manageable cyclicality, not terminal impairment — so this is not a value trap, just a fully valued quality cyclical at a cycle-favorable peak.

Verdict: pass for now, conviction 3 out of 5. A wonderful company to own roughly $150 lower. The watchlist target is a capital-spending scare flush toward roughly $650 to $700, where the quality franchise and the roughly $15.6 billion backlog would offer a real margin of safety. On the pure valuation lens (ignoring catalyst and macro regime), EMCOR trades modestly through fair value on a narrow moat — fair, not cheap — so the pure-value case is a hold, not a bargain. The widening-but-narrow moat and elite returns prevent a lower conviction score; the full multiple prevents a higher one.
interrogator_dossier:
EMCOR Group (EME) — Forensic Dossier
Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8, scale-out Basket C (Thermal), electrical and mechanical contractor, data as of 2026-06-22

EMCOR is a roughly $18.5 to $19.25 billion-revenue (2026 estimated) US specialty mechanical and electrical construction and facilities-services company. It is one of the purest publicly traded picks-and-shovels plays on the physical build-out of AI data centers: the electrical distribution, mechanical systems, HVAC, piping, and fire and life-safety systems inside the shell. Its placement in the thermal and electrical contractor sleeve is precisely correct — EMCOR builds the power and cooling guts of the hyperscaler facility. The forensic question is not whether the demand is real (it visibly is — remaining performance obligation up 32.9% year over year to a record $15.62 billion), but whether the quality of the franchise justifies the premium and whether the AI capital-spending optionality is already in the stock.

Narrative Arc
Across the last several quarters the EMCOR story has narrowed and intensified around one vector: AI and data-center infrastructure. Two to three years ago the narrative was a diversified, all-weather mechanical and electrical contractor — building-services annuity, industrial and refinery turnarounds, healthcare, commercial, plus a steady book of network work. The data-center and network-and-communications line was a contributor, not the headline. By the first quarter of 2026 it is the headline: chief executive Tony Guzzi frames 'unprecedented activity' driven by 'customer investments in AI infrastructure, cloud infrastructure,' with network and communications revenue up roughly 50% in Electrical and 86% in Mechanical year over year. The thesis shifted from 'durable diversified compounder' to 'diversified compounder with a hyperscaler-capex turbocharger.' Management has not abandoned the diversification framing — the right defensive posture for a cyclical contractor. The UK business was divested in the period, a quiet portfolio-pruning that sharpens the US data-center focus. The arc is expansion and confidence, not contraction.

Claims vs. Financials (Forensic Check)
Record revenue and broad-based strength: revenue $4.63 billion, up 19.7% year over year; organic growth 16.8% — verified (organic growth means real demand, not roll-up arithmetic). Data center is the driver — US Electrical up 33.1%, US Mechanical up 28.9%, network and communications up roughly 50% and 86% respectively — verified and then some. Margins improving — operating margin 8.7% versus 8.2%, operating income up 26.7% (faster than revenue) — verified; earnings per share up 30% exceeded revenue growth of 19.7%. Backlog inflection — remaining performance obligation $15.62 billion, up 32.9% year over year and $2.37 billion quarter over quarter, with roughly 78% converting within 12 months — verified, the single strongest data point. 'Strong and liquid' balance sheet — cash $916 million, total debt $6.1 million, debt-to-capital 0.2% — verified, pristine. Disciplined capital return — $105 million in buybacks and dividends in the first quarter; 51% of cash to shareholders since 2017 — verified. Mix headwind — Mechanical margin 10.9% versus 11.9% on guaranteed-maximum-price and cost-plus work in new geographies — honestly disclosed (a credibility green flag). Management's 'margin dollars over margin points' framing is a soft pre-emptive defense against percentage-margin dilution. Financials are ahead of the rhetoric; there is essentially no negative divergence. Return on invested capital of roughly 21 to 27% and free cash flow of roughly $1.34 billion (2024) are best-in-class for a contractor.

Tone and Confidence Trajectory
Rising. Guzzi's language is offensive in posture: 'We see no sign of slowing demand in this vertical.' Guidance was raised (revenue to $18.50 to $19.25 billion; earnings per share to $28.25 to $29.75). The one measured note is deliberately credible: supervision is the true bottleneck — 'We have to create more foreman' — the tell of a team that knows the constraint is execution capacity, not demand. Acknowledging geopolitical risks and rising commodity prices is seasoned realism, not a guide-down.

Guidance Credibility
High. EMCOR has a multi-year pattern of setting guidance then raising it; the first quarter of 2026 is the latest instance. The earnings-per-share beat (up 30% year over year, roughly 16% above the street's quarterly estimate) and revenue beat (roughly 10% above estimates) fit a sandbag-then-raise pattern. The 78% converts-in-12-months disclosure (down from 82%) is a candid, quantified caveat signaling the backlog tail is lengthening into 2027. Reliability: high.

Analyst Pressure Points
(a) Durability and cyclicality of the data-center capital-spending wave — management answers 'no sign of slowing' but cannot prove out-years. (b) Margin trajectory as mix shifts to guaranteed-maximum-price and cost-plus work — management deflects to 'margin dollars,' the persistent soft spot analysts keep pressing. (c) Customer concentration in hyperscalers — management asserts diversification but discloses no concentration number. (d) Valuation — most-pressed externally; the stock fell roughly 1.8% on the record print, the classic priced-in signal.

Red Flags and Green Flags
Green flags: organic growth of 16.8%; remaining performance obligation up 32.9% to a record $15.62 billion with roughly 78% near-term conversion; effectively net-cash (roughly $910 million, debt $6.1 million); return on invested capital of roughly 21 to 27% with expanding operating margin and earnings growth exceeding revenue growth (widening returns); honest, quantified disclosure of the margin headwind and the conversion-rate slip.

Red flags: Mechanical margin compressed 100 basis points on guaranteed-maximum-price and new-geography mix; hyperscaler customer concentration is not disclosed; valuation and positioning (the stock fell on a beat — AI capital-spending optionality is substantially priced in); single-factor dependence (hyperscaler AI capital-spending durability is the same shared driver as the rest of the basket, providing low independent diversification).

Hidden Signals
The shift from 'network and communications' to explicit 'AI infrastructure and cloud infrastructure' language signals deliberate leaning into the AI narrative. 'Margin dollars, not margin points' pre-positions investors for percentage-margin dilution as the company scales. The supervision-bottleneck comment means growth is now gated internally — a high-class problem but a real ceiling on bid-pipeline conversion. The UK divestiture with no strategic commentary signals pruning of low-return geographies to concentrate capital on the high-return US engine.

Capital-Allocation Assessment
Strengthening. EMCOR is executing a textbook high-return, net-cash, buyback-compounding contractor model directly in the path of the largest construction-capital-spending wave in a generation, with remaining performance obligation up 33% confirming demand is contracted, not hoped-for.

The most important thing to weigh: this is a great business at a full price whose key driver is the same AI capital-spending factor as the rest of the basket — the risk is (a) how much optionality is already in a roughly 26 times multiple after a 109% run, and (b) basket concentration on one shared resolution driver.

Moat: real but narrow and skill- and scale-based — scale, bonding capacity, execution reputation, and a deep supervision and union bench (the supervision bottleneck is the moat — not replicable overnight); switching-cost-light and project-re-compete on the construction side (building services are stickier). Widening in evidence terms (rising return on invested capital, expanding margins amid hyper-growth). The secular threat is cyclicality, not displacement — if AI capital spending pauses after 2027 the construction segments de-rate hard; manageable, not terminal (physical electrical and mechanical work cannot be automated away). EMCOR is not cheap; it is fairly to fully priced quality — the risk is a fully valued cyclical at a cycle-favorable moment.

Management credibility 5 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but real; moat trend widening; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: No kill fact found. Every load-bearing financial figure verifies against primary sources (SEC Form 8-K and Form 10-Q for the period ended 2026-03-31, EMCOR investor relations): revenue $4.63 billion (up 19.7%, organic up 16.8%), remaining performance obligation $15.62 billion (up 32.9%, up $2.37 billion quarter over quarter), diluted earnings per share $6.84 (up 30%), operating margin 8.7%, effectively net-cash (cash $916.4 million, only roughly $2.4 million of finance-lease obligations, $1.30 billion revolving credit facility undrawn), and full-year 2026 guidance raised to $18.50 to $19.25 billion revenue and $28.25 to $29.75 earnings per share. The bear thesis is a valuation, timing, and saturation argument, not a dispute about facts — and the facts support it: this is a fully valued quality cyclical whose sole resolution driver (hyperscaler AI capital-spending durability) is the same shared macro factor as the rest of the basket, with a narrow project-re-compete moat and a catalyst that has already fired (the stock fell on the record beat-and-raise). Nothing breaks the thesis.
skeptic_corrections: STALE PRICE ANCHOR: file uses current_price $845.43, but EME has since risen to ~$835-933 (stockanalysis.com $836.59 as of 2026-06-18; intraday quotes to ~$933) with a 52-week HIGH of $951.96. This pushes the stock even FURTHER above the $760-800 base-case fair value and toward its high — STRENGTHENING the priced-in bear case, not weakening it. The sop_fair_value ($760-800) is built on verified post-Q1-raise financials (NOT stale), so the SoP anchor is sound; only the live price needs refreshing.

OVERSTATED RALLY FIGURE: the dossier repeatedly cites a '~109% twelve-month rally' as the priced-in tell. Independent sources put the actual trailing-12-month return at ~67-88% (stockanalysis.com ~75%; 52-wk low $478.16). Still a very large run that fully supports the 'priced-in' read, but the specific +109% number is wrong and should be corrected to ~75% (~67-88% range).

MULTIPLE: file cites ~26x trailing / ~25x forward; live data shows trailing ~28x and forward ~26-28x (stockanalysis.com forward 28.21, trailing 28.07). The stock is modestly RICHER than the file states, again reinforcing (not undermining) the saturation/priced-in bear conclusion.
skeptic_evidence: SEC Form 8-K (EME exhibit 99.1, period 2026-03-31): Q1 2026 record revenue $4.63B (+19.7%, organic +16.8%); record RPO $15.62B (+32.9% YoY, +$2.37B QoQ); diluted EPS $6.84 (+30%); operating income $404M / 8.7% margin; FY2026 guidance raised to $18.50-19.25B revenue and $28.25-29.75 EPS (sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0000105634, filed Apr 2026).

SEC Form 10-Q (EME, period ended 2026-03-31): cash & equivalents $916.4M, no funded debt (~$2.4M finance leases only), $1.30B revolver undrawn — effectively net cash ~$914M, total shareholder equity ~$3.7B (sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0000105634/000010563426000046/eme-20260331.htm).

stockanalysis.com/stocks/eme (as of 2026-06-18): price $836.59, 52-week range $478.16-$951.96, ~12-mo return ~75%, forward P/E 28.21, trailing P/E 28.07, market cap $37.18B — confirms the name trades above the $760-800 fair value and near its 52-week high (priced-in).

Seeking Alpha / StockTitan / Investing.com (Apr 2026 Q1 2026 earnings recaps): corroborate Mechanical Construction margin 10.9% (down ~100bp from 11.9%), Electrical +33.1%, Mechanical +28.9%, data-center-led demand, and the raised FY2026 guide — confirming the bear's margin-mix and concentration concerns are real.

scale.role: Highest-quality mechanical/electrical contractor installing DC guts; RPO +32.9% to $15.62B.
scale.rationale: Elite contractor, RPO durable (+32.9% to $15.62B), effectively net cash — the best thermal-contractor franchise, a strong LEVER (the project-re-compete moat is narrower than a pure-play toll, so below CORE). Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, corrections are price/rally footnotes that REINFORCE the priced-in read (rally ~75% not ~109%; trades richer, near its 52-wk high), so a ~8pt haircut off a ~68 franchise. Catalyst FIRED (fell on the record beat-and-raise); size and wait for the ~$700-760 reset.
scale.demand_durability: High — DC mechanical/electrical install backlog, but broad non-residential construction mix.
